author: desolator_x
content: Something that popped into my mind when playing Rimworld: can we please have gnomes use stockpiles efficiently in the sense that they stack items to their max stack size instead of spreading 10 pieces of wood over as many tiles?

author: newboerg
content: hard to implement i guess, imagine a 63/64 Stack of wood on the other end of the map...

author: rauk
content: Maybe it would possible to turn this on / off for a specific stockpile

author: newboerg
content: in gnomoria you can do this using priorities

author: rauk
content: It can even be a "technology" (ie : you need to develop something to tie the planks together, like a string)

author: desolator_x
content: I dunno, there's a mod for it in Rimworld, so I guess it can be done. It's also *per stockpile* not global, so if a gnome brings, say, 10 wood to a stockpile with one 63/64 pile and two empty spaces it'd end up with a 64/64 and 9/64 pile ideally. since it's going to *that* stockpile the gnome wouldn't need to consider and other stockpiles with */64 wood stacks. Another option would be to have something like "tidying" as a seperate job, where gnomes would go around stockpiles and try to fill / combine stacks within that stockpile.

author: the_real_kyrros
content:
Not sure there's a lot of need for that... at least I haven't run in to any issue. They fill each spot, until it's full, then when it's full, they stack whatever stacks, rinse and repeat.
Does it set off OCD alarms in some people?... I'm sure it does.

author: the_real_kyrros
content: Also, saying that something can be done in 'Random game A' so it should be possible in 'Random Game B' is usually very not possible, especially if they're not even designed the same or use the same coding languages/engines.

author: .roest
content: Well they already stack stuff

author: .roest
content: a stockpile has a number of tiles, when there is no container an empty tile has a capacitiy of  1

author: .roest
content: now lets say 5 gnomes are idle and they ask for hauling jobs

author: .roest
content: so the stockpile goes over its tiles and checks if there is space, then looks if there are items that need hauling, then claims such an item and create the hauling job

author: .roest
content: now that stockpile tile is reserved for that item

author: .roest
content: items have a possible stacksize, meaning that a number of items of the same type may occupy the same tile up to that stacksize number, when an item is place at the stockpile, that stockpile tile now has the capacity of that

author: .roest
content: now can i already set the capacity of the stockpile tile to that number if the item is reserved?

author: .roest
content: so the next free tile creates a job to get the next item of the same type

author: .roest
content: now if the stockpile is full or no other items to haule out there gnomes will stack up items to their maximum stacksize thus freeing tiles

author: .roest
content: of course the stockpile code is still in flux with priorities and wheelbarrows still to be implemented

author: .roest
content: i'm very much against "tidying" jobs, a functioning stockpile algorithm should take care of that

author: desolator_x
content: Yeah, I agree with that one, that's why I'm so explicitly asking for it 😄 Maybe it could be build into the hauling job? Like, when the gnome gets to the stockpile and it has delivered the goods it immediately checks for a stacks in that stockpile that could be "tidied"

author: newboerg
content: maybe diffentiate between hauling and tidieing?

author: the_real_kyrros
content:
The issue, is that, functionally, stockpiles still work the same either way. 
*Use until full
*If full, organize/condense automatically to create more space (if available).
Granted, the OCD in all of us want to just take a filing cabinet to the whole thing to make it look nice and tidy... but it comes down to function vs form. Right now, it functions properly, even if it's messy graphically.

author: desolator_x
content: well, it would save some issues for people who don't want to make a million stockpiles for all the different goods. On that note: how many stockpiles can you link to a workbench? Since a high cap might limit the problem that comes from not stacking items in a stockpile that stores multiple items

author: the_real_kyrros
content: As far as I know, there's no limit... unless you're talking about the adjacent 3x3's... in which case, only one can feed, but it can feed from each of its 4 sides.

author: desolator_x
content: Yeah, and there's a problem right there with a 3x3 and unstacked tiles: if you need / to store a decent amount of 4-5 materials it's really hard to get that organized in a single storage area. It'd be a lot easier if you could have one workshop feed off of, say, 3 stockpiles who're all 3x1 and only contain 1, maybe 2 different materials

author: tacyn.
content:
That's getting too complicated. Both for reost to implement and debug as well as  for players to use.
The feed stockpile is just meant as a buffer for the most used inputs to save the crafter some legwork.
If you want to use more complex setups you should build dedicated workshops for each item.

author: desolator_x
content: well, it's mostly for things like ores and gems and such, I assume there's going to be quite a lot of them, and a 3x3 stockpile might not be able to hold all of the required inputs (since you need fuel as well) if a stockpile doesn't get stacked well and you end up with 3 piles of 1 piece of iron, instead of 1 stack of 3

author: desolator_x

roest already said that stockpiles get tidied up.

Especially since that one is bugged as hell.
For example, if stacks aren't forced to be the same material, the problem you mentioned doesn't occur.

author: guri9486
content:
An easier way to fit downwards.  In Gnomoria I found that I had to give the commends to dig, adding ramps to each level.
I don’t know how it should be done, but I do think that if you are digging down the 15 levels, there should be a way that you can do it without giving several commands on each floor.

author: guri9486
content: Also, I thought that the alarm bell mechanic was implemented in a way that’s punishingly confusing.  I think that you should either allow the bell to be rung by the player, or else give the bell its own priority so that we can make sure that ring a bell overrides every other action.

author: daniangione
content:
These things are not frowned upon as long as they're acknowledged as bad in the game itself; for example, you have slavery and cannibalism in RimWorld but you also have people (most) that get negative mood effects from these things, which represents that they are present due to the context of the game world but you're not encouraging nor saying they're good things, etc;
So I guess that as long as (for example) not every gnome accepts those things as "natural" - so using them has a "cost" instead of being free workers (in terms of gameplay) these things are acceptable in games and shouldn't be a problem

author: daniangione
content: Just my views on it :)

author: desolator_x
content: oh yeah, but in Rimworld mood is pretty important. In Ingnomia there's no mood system (yet?), so you'd have to add it just for the sake of slavery. That's why I don't like it as a system *atm* since there's more important stuff to focus on imo (and likewise I think moving it to a mod makes more sense since someone can then mod in the mood system separately)

author: daniangione
content: Yes, of course, I just assumed there would be something akin to mood or happiness at some point

author: daniangione

author: daniangione

content:
I beg forgivness, I've been reading a bucket load of Roomcarnage (DF story thing, epic, go google it) and have a few suggestions. I haven't gone through to see what other folks have sugessted so Imma just go ahead and put it out there

Something like Foul Fog; I love the aspect of having an almost ever present threat like the Foul Fog, omething that will turn dwarfs (or gnomes... or anything else) into a living hating being. I understand that, in DF, theres no wat to cure it, so maybe a workaround for something like Foul Fog and its problems.

The usual Zombies, Skeletons and so forth, raids that happen, though stagger them, have them in variable amounts or based on the total worth of your place, its an idea considering the coding that would have to be implimented for things like that.

UNDERGROUNNNNNND! Like any sensible Gnome, everyone knows that the safest place to be to prevent the sky from falling on your head, is in the ground, of course finding a cavern or cave is even better. (Caverns and caves)

I'll probably throw more up here, I noticed Procedural generation is something, will you maybe have a kind of 'fog of war' deal going on? Gnomes are kinda short and all, so not having a huge amount of sight is something, maybe shrubs and trees block LOS and hills make it impossible to know whats over there, thus the entire map isn't revealed in one hit

author: tacyn.
content:
I think light is badly used in Gnomoria. 
Setting up light sources is tedious and only done to prevent something even more annoying (random spawning enemies killing your civilians).
That's probably the real reason people complain about expensive torches or don't want light sources requiering fuel

author: dragonwrath420
content: Yeah, though I do have a small suggestion......

author: dragonwrath420
content: I would love to see different colors of lights if that's possible. Good example would be like in Terraria where you have all these different colors and then special lights that reach/light more than others (like the ultra-brights). Only thing I don't like about Terraria's lights/torches is that (when I last played), the different colors lit a different amount and there was no option to make them all light the same distance so some (like blue I believe), you had to use up to 3 times the amount to light the same area.

author: dragonwrath420
content: Also... <@358668557074300942> I have to say that I really love your modding work... I absolutely flat out refuse to play Gnomoria without your Modpacks :D... and I definitely look forward to seeing what you can do for ingnomia (already seen your seasons sprite mods, looks great!).

author: tacyn.
content:
If light is just aesthetic, then permanent light sources are fine. 
But, if light has a proper use that adds to the game rather than detract from it,
it should also have an appropriate cost such as requiring fuel and/or refills.
The key would be that the benefit is high enough that you want to use it sometimes,
but not so high that it is always optimal. 
Such decisions are, I think, a core of this type of game.

author: dragonwrath420
content: In that case I would give the ones that would require fuel and/or refills to do something "extra" such as light an area 2 to 5 times larger lit radius for example, or for things like campfires which can give a boost to certain skills such as cooking (but not make it required, just an added benefit).

author: tacyn.
content: Not a bad idea to have separate types of light with costs appropriate to their effects.

author: dragonwrath420
content: Another is that some can give a passive benefit such as allowing crops to continue to grow at night (since it was mentioned earlier that crops won't continue to grow during the night time), a buff of some kind (such as better mood or slower decrease in mood/sleep). Could provide a boost to certain skills while within range such as to mining, combat, fine intricate work (jewelry making for example), (slightly) faster movement, etc. Mind you that all would be minor but just enough to be worth it for those that want those extra boosts.

author: dragonwrath420
content: Exactly.... If that's possible then that would be amazing. If each type of light can also have a variety of colors then that would be even better for various reasons such as stylized "mood" lighting, or even being able to color-code areas via light.

author: dragonwrath420
content: Just some thoughts.

author: ctherain
content: dyed hot pink torches

author: ctherain
content: could use pink slime as a starter

author: tacyn.
content:
Aside from what <@297781221750996992> mentioned, my suggestions are:
A type of enemy that reacts to light (Can't enter lit areas, is hurt by light or is only vulnerable in the light)
Certain jobs/workshops requiring light to function (so you can use them only in daylight or need to produce the fuel to use them inside)
Underground farms/pastures that allow growing above-ground or special plants

author: dragonwrath420
content:
The effect on enemies would be rather interesting and to add an option of a setting to make certain enemies stronger while in light (for the more diehard fans that like the challenges).

I like that though I would change the certain jobs requiring light to function to using the light to increase production instead, this way no one gets annoyed/pissed off about the requirements on top of what may already be there.

I agree with the underground farms being able to grow above ground but I would flip that as I would think it'd make more sense to allow the above ground farms to be able to be grown underground with the lights. However being able to do it both ways would be great to allow for even greater customization/options/choices of how to build your kingdoms.

author: not__
content:
If the plan is to have eldritch horrors in the game, bright light should be a way to either reduce sanity loss (assuming sanity is a stat), prevent it entirely, or restore it once away from the effects of insanity.
DF has, I think, a system whereby the dwarfs get less tolerant of the outdoors the less they're in it.  In a similar vein, perhaps if the player chooses a completely subterranean life,  Ingnomia's gnomes become more, let's say, Morlock-like, preferring the underground and gaining certain changes in physical and other characteristics, but being actively harmed by light.  Maybe this can be a start setting as a permanent choice.  Couple that with types of monsters that prefer light or dark, the game's opponents could have different strategies depending on whether you're running a batch of light-tolerant or light-shunning gnomes.

author: optimus.0001
content:
Let me regurgitate some thoughts all over the nice clean channel.


Magic candles/lights would need a source of power, as would other magic elements in the base.
There's also the notion of using magic as offensive skills or buffs eg insta-harvest... These could come from a common resource (say, mana).

Mana gets generated through some gnomes being given a profession and working a workshop or room eg shaman gnomes in a rock shrine, the greater value the shrine the higher the resource generation modifier.

Balance wise, it could be stockpiled but if used offensively during a goblin wave, it should be a choice between saving from slaughter but the magic devices in the kingdom suffering afterwards from a lack of mana. Or the kingdom suffering while all gnomes go shaman chanting for a week.

author: .roest
content: that hurts my feelings

author: .roest
content: also for every tile visible it renders two quads a.k.a. 4 triangles, which is very fast since it's instance based rendering, the fragment shader does the heavy lifting here

author: .roest
content: now if you get constantly bad fps it could mean the shader processing is slow on your grpahics card, the world is too big so not everything is in graphics card memory and it uses shared memory

author: .roest
content: you are zoomed out too far, gnomoria has a max zoom level, i don't limit it

author: .roest
content: your view level is higher than the plane you're looking at so it processes all the tiles there too

author: .roest
content: other than that i have no idea than ati/amd is just bad for gaming and it reinforces my dislike 😃

author: chaineddjinn
content:
I find it funny how when I explain my fort ideas to some people, that I keep the Nobles separate from the rest of the population. They think I'm doing it because the Nobles don't need to sully themselves interacting with riffraff...

This is not the case.

As long as my nobles are away from the general population, it's easier to mitigate damages when they suddenly disappear/expire.

author: chaineddjinn
content: On that note, can Nobles be something you can choose is allowed in each games instance?

author: .alch
content:
any auto prioritization for large scale dig/build orders?

An example problem I've had, building filling a 1 wide tunnel and a gnome getting stuck because they worked on filling the end of the tunnel while another filled the entrance.

Or a large dig floor order and gnomes getting stranded in an island in the middle since the outside was dug out whilst a few worked on the centre.

Some system where the furthest job from the point of entry is active whilst rest are inactive

author: desolator_x
content: just like in Dungeons 3, sounds like a good idea to implement

author: .roest
content: this is usually the time where I tell you it's not that simple

author: .alch
content: Wouldn't think it's that simple to code in, easiest way that jumps to mind would be a some job setting to only has active the job that's furthest in terms of walking distance from the first gnome that starts working on it. That way something like a large dig floor order would avoid gnomes getting stranded

author: .alch
content: It's a pretty minor thing, just easier than creating line by line dig jobs.

author: .alch
content: Already had a gnome fall through the floor once xD

author: .roest
content: well it's really not simple and it's even harder if there are more than one gnomes working on jobs, so yes there will be something at some point that helps with that but it will probably never 100% ensure you don't trap gnomes

author: .roest
content: about gnomes falling down when you remove floor, i'm a bit undecided about that, the remove floor job is done from a neighboring tile, so instead of removing the floor and letting the gnome that is on the tile fall down, we could evacuate him to the tile the worker is on

author: .alch
content: Something to add when a gnome falls, the location they fall to doesn't seem to become discovered, so if they fall into a wall tile, it stays as an undiscovered wall and same with the surrounding area.

author: .roest
content: k, that's fixed now

author: fatmck
content: Wouldn't it be some kind of solution if we could check something like "build in order" before we draw a line of wall or filling a hole? So if we want a 13 tile long fill job of a 1 wide tunnel we check it and draw it from exit to entrance. It would then offer only the first wall as job to the gnomes. when this is done it gives the job for the second tile. Or in other words, it 'suspends' all jobs but the first drawn, when its finished .. you get the idea .. you are the smart guy here. 😉

author: tacyn.
content:
The designation would be the same as other rooms.
Comparable to dormitories, which allow gnomes to use the beds build within them.
The crafting screen is the same as for single workshops, with the difference that it can do everything the workshops within the designation can.
The orders created in that crafting screen are then synchronized  with the appropriate workshops.

author: tacyn.
content:
Have you considered using behavior trees for the ai? For example, this free library at https://github.com/BehaviorTree/BehaviorTree.CPP.
It allows loading behavior-trees as xml at runtime. With this, the AI could be moddable.
And even without making it moddable, this might make it easier to develop and debug the ai.

The gnome behavior in regular play is probably simple enough for a hard-coded version to be manageable.
But once combat is introduced, it could get messy. For example, fighting in squads, recovering/treating injured gnomes, enemy types with unique behavior.

author: yksleets
content: 2 things I thought of as suggestions while trying to persuade a 6 year old to sleep.

author: yksleets
content: 1) Some sort of a blueprint system, maybe along the lines of Factorio where you have to build it once but then you can make a copy to a book you can plop down items from as needed. I like to build my core the same way each time so having an easy way to recreate it woudl be amazing. Maybe even a way to export it as a string to be sent to a friend to play with.

author: yksleets
content: 2) Something along the lines of the manager in Dwarf Fortress where you can queue up a number of a specific item and it gets made by whichever shop has the capacity first. We already have the "Keep 10 planks in stock" in the workshops directly but a way to send in a build order without going to the specific shop would be great.

author: chaineddjinn
content:
<@207714562437414912> 
Sounds a lot similar to what the devs want to make with StoneHearth
https://store.steampowered.com/app/253250/Stonehearth/

I agree, it would be interesting and add replayability.
Let's get the game out of the ugly duckling stage first, there's a lot that needs to be cleaned and optimized before we can get that far.

One thing I would like to see in the near future;
**Backpacks**
I don't mean like 'bags' and 'sacks' that work like barrels and wheelbarrows.
I mean extra storage of certain items and customized in 'gear' setting.
For instance, a guard patrolling can carry more food and drink so he doesn't go running back inside wearing himself out.
Someone building a structure won't drop what their doing to grab a drink, just 'pause' the job temporarily.
And the best part.
Field medics.
One of your stumpy's gets injured on the frontline, what typically happens?
Goes running with a blood trail to the nearest bandage pile.
What if instead he moves to the back of a 'formation' to get treated by the doc? This would give a use to the "Doctoring" skill and better doc's heal faster.
Maybe injury wouldn't be fully healed on the battle field but would improve mortality rates.
Just an idea I had after a fight with my first ogre.

author: chaineddjinn
content:
Backpacks would also allow for 'Expeditions' and the bigger the pack, the more they can explore.
Maybe instead of 'waiting' for migrants you can go out and find some?
Would allow for getting more gnomes with less KW as it's a risk sending out people.

author: chaineddjinn
content: Think it would be a fun option worth exploring, but that's just my stance.

author: chaineddjinn
content:
Maybe eventual 'carts' could be made? Or different kinds of 'Pack animal' for longer distance...
but I'm not gonna wager that far, keep things simple for now while laying a solid foundation.

No, Wheelbarrows move resources like stones and stuff into piles.
When building something, a Gnome will grab each part one at a time and if ANYTHING happens; Sees a lizard, gets thirsty or sleepy They quit the job and then another Gnome comes and takes all the items one. by. one. back to the pile they came from.
What I was meaning, is when a Gnome gets a building job, they get a backpack, put all the parts FOR the thing into it and THEN go to build. Thus reducing time wasted.
Wheelbarrows don't seem to do anything for building.

author: chaineddjinn
content:
I'm sorry but in the convo above <@202164327606648841> is **SUPER** aggro toward @derPaddy#0188 just because he had a difference in opinion.

I feel surface stone would also prove useful as eventually their may be a point system which is costly to bring stone at embark or may have other restrictions like weight.
Another point is when enemies are going to be implemented, are they going to spawn from the dark like in Gnomoria? I've stayed on the upper layers in my games because everytime I go over depth 5+ zombies and skellies zurg rush the hell out of my bases. 
Current base(in Gnomoria) is over 5 Years near surface so only problem seems to be underground.
Surface stone would also be more realistic as their are mountains and quarries which have supplied stone for castles in ages past.
There is also the idea that certain stone can be used for certain things; Obsidian can be used to make sharper stone weapons, Cinnabar makes great stone fall traps or bauxite/cobalt is good lava-proof stone, etc. This makes different stone have more value for various projects.
The whole 'Deal with it because nothing you say matters' kinda detracts from the purpose of this server in a way, which I thought was feedback and ideas.
Speaking of thinking, nice to know that, and I quote;
"basically everyone here except for you knows that this is going to be a Gnomoria+"
And here I had hoped that it would be more of a bridge between the two.
Granted it's near the beginning but if all you aspire to is just where the game collapsed + a couple extras, I feel a little disappointed.

author: chaineddjinn
content:
(Not that many would give a rats rump, but that's not the point.)
I had felt this would aspire to include some of the function and versatility of where the predecessor of this got it's life from, one reason a lot of people supported Gnomoria was because some people who like DF wanted ladder to learn more ropes to get better at it by playing things similar.

I've said my opinion and keep my eye on how things develop, I just hope it can go farther then some think it will.
(Please make modding easy, it'll keep the game alive if nothing else.)

author: desolator_x
content:
I don't want to continue the discussion on derpaddy. He didn't even read the steam page, as such he comes here with a totally uninformed opinion. The whole point of this discord, especially the suggestions channel, is to provide informed and constructive criticism. If you cannot even take the smallest effort to inform yourself before you come here you have no place to start demanding things. Furthermore you *will* get a snappy response, either from me, or from Roest. As you all know I'm totally open to discuss basically everything in here as long as people come with an informed argument and some explanation on how it relates to Gnomoria features, how it'll fit into Ingnomia, and how it'll be worth the time investment. 
Should you have a personal problem with how I do things, feel free to PM me and we can talk about it.

on the matter of surface stone: Surface stone  =/= mountain or quarry. These things are either steep hills or valleys and those already modify the height at which stone spawns (mountain) or lay stone bare (valley), so what you describe is technically already in the game

-Suggestion: Is it possible to add and option to stockpile limits to count items of any/selected materials?

For instance, I`m crafting statuettes from orange and pine planks (order with any materials, whatever is available) and I'm storing them at a stockpile but I don't want my haulers running there all the time. content:
Just final comment on limiting by materials: another use case of limiting stockpiles with different materials is while using it with linked stockpiles. You can't have 2 linked stockpiles on one workshop. Hence, having one linked stockpiles with different materials limits solves that issue if you intend to have mixed crafting materials.

However, I think that it is better to have limits relating to the total items of same type, as there are more uses for this situation, rather than limiting by materials. And you can always have 2 or more workshops, each with different linked stockpiles with single materials limit.

author: fatmck
content:
Suggestion for Needs / Mood

Just read the wiki and read about Needs. Beside hunger, thirst and sleep I would add comfort and satisfaction. Propably they are more moods then needs, depending on how mood will be implemented into the game.

Comfort and satisfaction wouldn’t be necessary at the beginning, but could also be a requirement for being more efficient later and maybe raising the possibility to create better quality stuff.

Parts of comfort would be the overall kingdom worth, the personal room of a gnome and personal stuff of a gnome, for example clothes*.

*Clothes:
Additional I suggest clothes for gnomes. Its questionable if gnomes should get additional cloth slots or if clothes are just additional armor pieces (with minimal to no armor). For the beginning it could be straw heads, fabric shirts and trousers, wooden shoes. Later felt or leather hats, silk shirts and trousers, leather shoes. Clothes would wear off over time so that there should always be new ones to pick up. Additionaly tailoring would getting more important.

Satisfaction would be primary food and drinks* but maybe also social interaction (possible when eating at the great hall together with other gnomes for example)

*Food/Drinks:
Unless food and drinks wouldn’t be balanced with rocket science formulas there will probably always be the best food and drinks which gets produces endless towards endgame. To loosen that up gnomes could generally like some kinds of food/drinks more then other but would prefer diversity for better satisfaction. So no matter how much a gnome loves legendary sandwiches with yak sausage, egg, tomatos and onion .. at some point they would be over eaten of it and prefer anything else. This would also give us a real reson to vary between all those plants and animals instead of using only a minimum.

author: chaineddjinn
content:
<@202164327606648841> Sorry just got back to this.

The problem with that "cost" is it's easily subverted if your clever.
I put most of my people in a group and start training them asap, after initial set up.(1st day forging/logging/mining)
I put 1 miner, farmer and anyone non-essential into 2 groups, when one works, the other trains.
I think I'm on year 2 of gnomoria and my strongest "fighters"  have CQC around 100 with dodge half that.
(Edit; Talkin' bout gnomoria here^)

I was just mentioning because it takes so long to get 1 point, but you'ed need something to justify the quicker learning.
Also, bandages should not heal everything like a magic Band-Aid solution. That should be what higher level potion/spells are for...

<@177094350201683968> <@386915067477753858> 
As for backfiring spells? ehhhh, Only excuse i can think of is if the gnomes teeth are knocked out, causing him to Sylvester the spell up his own @#$.
Other then that, I'd pass. If I work my way up to high level magic, it better work like high level magic.

author: desolator_x
content:
obviously you don't need to be without workers doing certain jobs. I'm not that dumb that I'd be without miners, ever (or anything else essential for that matter). That doesn't take away from the fact that you still lose half you manpower to training. In gnomoria the cost of that was that you could easily skip ahead to the automated defences of walls, pits with archers, or anything else of your choice. 
Way of the gnome training (which is the only viable thing you can get on day two) is nice for a while, but you still need to swap over to proper armour / weapons skills to train later in the game, which kinda makes early training redundant. 
At that point you're spending such an inordinate amount of time to train a squad that you're much better off just setting up automated defences anyway.

author: desolator_x
content:
if Roest doesn't want that to happen in this game as well I feel that the non magic combat training needs to be sped up considerably, if anything, compared to gnomoria just to be able to compete. 
This is further exacerbated by the fact that losing a single high skilled gnome in the mid - late game can have such a massive impact on your defensive capabilities that you can easily spiral downwards into losing more and more defenders with little possibility to get out of it without, once again, falling back to automated defenses.

author: desolator_x
content: Again, if the use of automated murder corridors is the way the game is supposed to function in the mid - late game and take over from armoured gnomes holding the line, this is completely fine. But if the game should support both playstyles the training time definitely shouldn't be longer than they currently are

author: chaineddjinn
content:
<@202164327606648841> PFFFT!
Ummm, I have no idea how you built your fort... but I haven't implemented a single automation and I believe I have yet to lose a single Gnome.

I put everyone in the millitary, **EVERYONE** even my tailor knows how to knock a lizards teeth out... or use their teeth to rip out it's heart.
Yeah, it took a while to get them that strong. I play for the long game most of the time anyway.(Keep KW as low as possible, only as much food/drink as needed for winter, leave any and all ore's unmined for later collection.)
As most of the tech needed to DO anything like that is locked behind the tinker tree, why rush when that'll only piss off your enemies?
As for my fort's defense, I go with the spiral pattern. A long maze that most enemies' have to path through before they get CLOSE to my base. I have an alternate spiral outward for my marksGnomes

author: chaineddjinn
content:
Not only is this great for defense but also hunting, as animals get thirst and run the gauntlet, they alert dwarves so I can move 1 ranged squad in front of it followed by another behind. All that's left is a little micromanagment to pincer it into submission an boom! Leather for armor! Meat for sausage and bones for figurines!
Even if the thing survives I have a platoon of leather armored Gnomes that have 2 fighter's with 2 bucklers each, both using taunt. An axemen who using 'cut them down' and swordsmen with the 'sneaky' perk allowing them to do more damage when not the target.
Behind those guys I have my trainee's who bite, kick and bludgeon anything they get close too!
I thought this game was about strategy, if I wanted automation I'd have bought 'facturio' or one of the games in that line of thought.
I mean sheesh! My KW in my recent play through only just broke 30K and I killed my first Ogre with my squads alone!
I'm having difficulty setting up the automation stuff, I'm still trying to connect my powersource to my trickle feeder that crosses close to my archery range.(Had to set them up out there, took too long for them to respond when they were inside)
I mean, I just feel generally confused.
Have I been playing the game the wrong way? I DUNNO!
Just a little flabbergasted at the moment I guess

author: ext3h
content:
Assigning uniforms to professions would be quite helpful. Currently, you can only assign an uniform implicitly by including a Gnome in a squad. IIRC, then Gnomoria had the option to assign a default Uniform per profession. If the Gnome is in both a profession and a squad defining a uniform, then squad has precedence.
In Gnomoria, this was essential for professions such as miners, as they frequently encountered enemies while mining. (E.g. miner by default in plate armor, haulers by default in light leather etc. was a sensible choice.)

author: ext3h
content: No tiles considered until candidates are found.

content: well, if you can make a distance check between two units, just by looking up their coordinates in world, then I'm thinking that could be faster

content:
Reminds me, there is also an Action missing for kiting movement. Actually two of them. One for finding random, safe tile within distance X, one for finding safe tile within distance X, *without* breaking LOS to current combat target. First for fleeing in non aggressive units, the second one for ranged combat units.

Looks like the second one is a little bit complicated though, as it requires tracking combat and movement targets individually.

author: ext3h
content: Finding safe position is simple. Find visible enemies first. From all found enemies simultaneously, flood map (BFS, no A*) to create threat assessment gradient map within X tiles distance of each enemy. Then BFS with limited depth from searching unit to find *local* threat minimum. (Move along same threat level, don't go to higher threat level. Choose random of found lowest.)

author: ext3h
content: Local minimum means you can't run through an enemy, if the safest position is opposite to him.

author: ext3h
content:
Balancing question, should plants keep growing / animal keep giving birth / laying eggs / beehives keep producing honey / trees growing if there is any obstacle (items, animals, gnomes) on the same tile?

Asking with regard to the common issue of seeds, fruits and eggs stacking to stacks of 100+ on farms, animals reproducing to the point where there is on average >2 animals per tile.

Not just for the sake of requiring the user to keep farms, pastures and groves tidy, but also to limit the number of items floating around.

Gnomoria had Golems to punish users for such behavior, everything not tidied up would become self-aware and start attacking the player. But even Gnomoria did usually not form stacks on farms etc.

author: ext3h
content:
You wouldn't use it though, point is, for all the resources which can be *harvested*, harvesting is so much faster than consumption. And for eggs etc., they just keep producing without any Gnomes being involved (spoiler, the auto-stop limits don't work properly for several reasons). If you would use the resources, you would never end up at the point where *food* starts stockpiling on a farm. Had let it kept running for a few ingame-years, and the production was simply excessive to the point where it's easier to let stuff out to rot.

Compare to how it worked in Gnomoria, at initial skill level, farming, work on the grove etc. was all so painfully slow, that you (initially) didn't ran into issues. Until players eventually figured out how to survive more than a year or two, and increment in skill level increased production by so far, that resources also started stockpiling in the wild. Introduction of Golems was necessary to keep the number of items scattered around in the range of a few thousand, rather than going into millions. Just to hide from the player that they were about to break the simulation.

Even traders only have access to stockpiled items. And stuff like seeds are actually unsellable, as they have a value of 0.

Just to make perfectly clear, a player who is cleaning up in the world would never even run into the case that production stops. Or wouldn't notice. Everything just keeps running smoothly, and without lags.

This is more about the players who don't like to invest Gnome work power into stockpiling (and resources into constructing stockpiles), and not by choice, just unintentionally, keep leaking items they don't even remember about and which they are never going to utilize.

So the intention is really to teach the player to clean up, right from the start. Before they unintentionally break the simulation at some point.

Providing the user with the means of mass item consumption is just the second step.

author: ext3h
content: Even if you provide the means, the user will do an evaluation of the cost-effectiveness of these means. And despite being an absolute necessity, mass trash item deletion can't possibly be allowed to be a prime income source.

author: .elfu
content: You know, there's prospector, that gives the ability to at least process trash dirt and similar raw materials. When it comes to food items, these could get made into a compost or fertilizer, and in later game, maybe fuel for machines/golems. Other than that an item could simply decay into a pile of trash, which, in effect, could cause gnomes to fall sick. That alone could be a good incentive to clean up. That and inevitable damage to surrounding area - make floor go bad, mutate, make it spew out hideous monsters, and be really hard to heal.

author: .elfu
content: My bet is still in having a chain of crafting that at the end creates an item that is widely consumable. Imagine Factorio, where everything is constantly in use, to create space research packs, which later on are used for continuous research.

author: .elfu
content: Maybe alchemy? Various organic products could be utilised there too.

author: ext3h
content: Factory has significant cost to every item you produced. And production lines which *always* stall back to the source if not fully consumed. Only exception are bot networks with storage chests, but that's 1:1 equivalent to stockpiles, and the items remain accessible.

author: .elfu
content: It doesn't change the fact that excess items get used. Make it use 10 seeds to make one bottle of oil, and you got yourself a pretty big sinkhole right there. And that's a start. If you come up with a nice chain, you can make it not stall at all, or maybe even overuse some materials, making you be careful on what you use your materials.

author: .elfu
content: Fuel for golems is just one example. Anything works as long as it allows materials to vaporize yet be useful in the process.

author: ext3h
content:
Excess item's don't get used though unless the contribute to some of the primary production chains. E.g. prospector just works, as it is a viable source of additional metal, in a production chain which is starved on inputs.

And even for the prospector, not many players actually discovered it, I suppose.

author: .elfu
content: Probably. It's there though. Even if it supplies a trade that has little resources. Other overflowing materials don't need to supply already saturated markets, if you create new ones, or come up with ways to support the old ones. I'm thinking both this and trash/decay system would certainly make the world cleaner.

author: ext3h
content: Either way, unless the player actually consumes, there has to be either a stall a some point, or a forced "self-cleanup" at some point, for technical reasons. Once there is, players are required to discover and utilize any of the potential methods.

So I felt the game was a little too quiet, and decided to throw this together and see how it feels playing over the game. If you dig it, I can mix/master it all together better but this is just a rough draft for the moment.

content: I certainly like the style.

author: ext3h
content: I'm just still trying to wrap my head around how to handle not the main menu, but the In-game music. As we are going to need some situation-awareness there.

author: ext3h
content: So I was thinking about a set of rythm parts, a couple of alternativeharmonics and melody for each rythm sets, which are shuffled based on in-game events, and then some additional ambient sounds mixed in as additional percussion

author: ext3h
content: Just some very coarse procedural sound

author: defox241
content: So like 2 or 3 short 1-2 bar segments that can play into eachother?

author: ext3h
content: Longer segments. Up to 20-30 seconds

author: defox241
content: Ahh alright, gotcha

author: ext3h
content: E.g. rythm theme "battle", melody "ogre King", ambient SFX "sword", "armor", "cry"

author: defox241
content: I can also make SFX

author: ext3h
content: Ambient SFX switching often (1-2 bars fits) and shuffled aggressively as camera changes, melody alternating frequently, rythm changing only every few minutes

author: ext3h
content: We would have to see how much "full" SFX we need and want in the first place. Not that we fall in the trap that after giving the first couple of events SFX, that all the ones not having any suddenly start sticking out.

author: ext3h
content: Or to phrase it differently: whether we want to condition users to play by ear at all.

- areas:
Place areas on ramps

- trees:
Merge nearby tree leaves (see image for orange trees)

Cut leaves without felling the entire tree.

author: dinosaure.plz_nofriendrequests
content:
While I'm in the suggestion channel:
- Have a "paint" mode, similar to the "Cut trees" one, but targeting farm/grove/pasture type helping changing seeds en masse
- Have an "any" mode on farm/grove/pasture to let them accept any seed/seed(/acorn/pine)/animal available
- Name the personalised "Set up" preset(s)
- Add a slight border around zones, to visually distinguish them
- Add some Z-layer "pollution" when generating layers (-> have 1 or 2 sandstone visible on the surface even when the start flatness is 0%, that could help making the first workshops, and vary the undergrounds, and ease new player experience tempting them to look at undergrounds)
- Add a "follow gnome" camera option
- Inform what kind of trader has arrived ("An [animal] merchant", "A [military] merchant", ...)
- Show Gnomes/Animals states in a panel
- Show Gnomes/Animals/Resources history panel
- Engage more than 1 gnome for some heavy jobs, the more gnomes the faster
- Show oak acorns and pine cones in seed category, along with willow and birch seeds in the "Set up" and "Stockpiles" page
- Make the game less generous in seed quantities
- Implement a decay system
- Disallow selection of stockpile filter for containers not allowing such category (namely "seeds" on crates-only stockpile)
- Extend containers max value above 255
- Don't bother me with New Gnome arrivals, I want an option to automatically proceed with gnome arrivals:
-- Refuse all requests
-- Accept all requests
- Automatic "Remove plants/trees/blocks" job when placing a building on top of them
- Add more starting drinkable things (fruits and vegetables)
- Add leaves/trunk/root "blocks" to place (and consider them as decorative only)
- Revamp trees as the sum of more smaller entities (trunk, leaves, roots)
-- Vary tree heights and shapes
-- Join nearby leaves
-- Expanding tree branches
- Add a "move this pending job to another workbench" button
- Collect seeds from trees, without felling them

- Add some fun, too.

author: maveross
content: Should get a "suggestions" page on github to comment on suggestions, just to help keep track of them and the discussions around each of them

author: ext3h
content:
Issues page should stay reserved for stuff which has been adopted by a developer or modder. There is just no point to flood it with 10+ new "suggestion" issues per day which have no chance of getting implemented before sliding off to page 10+
Some threaded form would be appropriate though, for ongoing discussions till a suggestion is actually fleshed out.

author: maveross
content: That's what I was thinking

author: ext3h
content: Just to avoid further misunderstandings, when I said "threaded" I meant "one thread per suggestion".  Because most of these "one liners" are still far from a state where they could "just be implemented", but each single one requiring a decent amount of planing and careful consideration about details before development work can even start.

author: ext3h
content: If anything, the project boards in GitHub, a Trello or just a plain forum would be better fits for that.

author: ext3h
content: That still requires some moderation though. Not because of blatant spam, but simply to focus attention (for the sake of getting feasible ideas into final state first), and to avoid duplicates.

author: maveross
content: I would be willing to moderate/help moderate to some extent. I work full time so wouldn't be able to do as comprehensive a job as others might

author: condac
content: Soo i thougt the food values where off, but I got the answer that happyness was a upcomming feature that will offset time spent on making food for the price of actual hunger values going down compared to values of the ingredients. But I have but togheter a small sheet of values where the total amount of food is kept and for each crafting step a bonus is added. I do not know the time frame for the happyness to be implemented but this might be a good temporary balance until then. Also i have included some experiments where crafting time is summed up as happyness for upcoming version.

author: .roest
content: looks good though that last value is a bit  off, i want to avoid a stituation like in Gnomoria where a single food item can keep them satisfied for several days,

author: .roest
content: 75 may be a good value for the best value and the rest balanced around that

author: condac
content: I also feared that it gets to high on some foods. Lowering the values of the raw materials will increase the difficulty early game but that might not be bad after all. Also splitting the result into two portions might be a second alternative and when we get the happyness system the splitted parts might keep the same happyness doubling that effect

author: condac
content: I looked at the code and as it is now max hugner is 150 and with eating scheduel gnomes will eat under 90% and any food item over 60 will just waste the food value

author: condac
content: I have extended the sheet with crafting time so we can set a price per work time to get calculated cash value of items based on ingredients crafting times. So if someone wants to grind some kind of production to sell to traders the effort is based on time to create items instead of just random numbers

author: hughheggen
content: will there be a mechanic to make meals a bit dynamic in their values?

author: condac
content: I am just creating a sheet to calculate everything based on the ingreidients because i thought the current values where just very fluctuating in value and eatvalue and did not favour crafting any items at all, at current state eating raw foods is better because they loose value the more you craft them

author: hughheggen
content: They should gain value

author: condac
content: Yes, that is what i think also, but the anwer was that they are planing on implementing happyness so the more you make a high end item the happier the gnomes will be eating it and the eatvalue will decrease, but it is not implemented yet, so i think a simple logic based system that grants some bonus for crafting things should be implemented in the mean while

author: condac
content: Can you explain a bit more what you mean by making the meals dynamic?

author: hughheggen
content:
So I've spent a bit of time playing Ingnomia recently and I've come up with these suggestions for improvements:

Merge harvest tree and forage into one command just called forage. There's little point in having them separate.

Allow Kitchens to turn seeds into flour at a rate of 4 or 5 seeds for 1 flour. It's a good use for excess seeds

Keep the job blocks brightly coloured even when in the dark. It's hard to tell the difference between a job and a workshop in the dark.

Add a wooden needle to the carpenter. Getting bone can take a while if you don't start with any.

author: chaineddjinn
content:
Another Idea I had was the ability to have more then one unit preform a task, like a cooperation feature; two working to build something would take less time then one on it's own.
Also, if someone is gathering resources in a location with the intent to build, don't have gnomes stash stuff away just because the one building got tired/hungry/thirsty/ect.
Could make a "construction site" that stocks the items used to build it.
Maybe have a "Contemplation/chill" mode whenever they finish a task? Like when a person moves a couch and steps back to admire their work. Those in that mode would take on other jobs nearby or finish a task someone else got distracted from instead of just grabbing stuff to store away.
